meta.title: AWS-DEA-1: Kinesis, Glue, Redshift and Lake Formation

questions.stem: A company needs to ingest clickstream data from a website at 100,000 events per second with sub-second latency for real-time analytics. Which AWS service is best suited for this ingestion layer?

options.text: Amazon Kinesis Data Streams
Amazon SQS
AWS Database Migration Service (DMS)
Amazon S3 with event notifications

questions.explanation:
Amazon Kinesis Data Streams is designed for real-time, high-throughput data streaming at massive scale (up to millions of records per second). It provides sub-second latency and enables multiple consumers to process the same stream. SQS is a message queue suitable for decoupling services but not designed for high-throughput real-time streaming analytics. DMS is for database migration, not streaming ingestion.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/streams/latest/dev/introduction.html

questions.stem: An AWS Glue ETL job fails with OutOfMemoryError when processing a 500 GB Parquet file from S3. The job uses DynamicFrames. What is the most effective solution?

options.text: Enable job bookmarks to skip already-processed records
Increase the number of DPUs (data processing units) allocated to the Glue job
Switch from DynamicFrames to RDD operations
Use Glue Streaming instead of batch processing

questions.explanation:
AWS Glue scales by allocating more DPUs (Data Processing Units). Each DPU provides 4 vCPUs and 16 GB memory. Increasing DPUs provides more total memory and parallelism to handle the 500 GB file. Job bookmarks skip previously processed data (useful for incremental loads, not OOM). Switching to RDD does not inherently solve memory issues. Glue Streaming is for continuous ingestion, not large batch file processing.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/author-job.html

questions.stem: You need to process streaming data from Kinesis Data Streams, apply transformations, and deliver results to Amazon Redshift and Amazon S3 simultaneously. Which service handles fan-out delivery with built-in transformations?

options.text: Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose (Amazon Data Firehose)
Amazon Kinesis Data Analytics
AWS Lambda with Kinesis trigger
Amazon MSK (Managed Streaming for Apache Kafka)

questions.explanation:
Amazon Data Firehose (formerly Kinesis Data Firehose) is a fully managed service that reads from Kinesis Data Streams, optionally transforms data via Lambda, buffers, and delivers to multiple destinations including S3, Redshift, OpenSearch, and Splunk. Multiple delivery streams can read from the same Kinesis stream. Kinesis Data Analytics performs SQL/Flink analytics but does not natively fan out to Redshift and S3 simultaneously without additional integration.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/streams/latest/dev/introduction.html

questions.stem: An AWS Glue job reads from a JDBC source and writes to S3 in Parquet format. After several months, you notice that the number of output Parquet files is very large (500,000+ small files). How should you reduce the file count?

options.text: Enable Glue job bookmarks to consolidate output files
Call coalesce() or repartition() on the DynamicFrame before writing to reduce the number of Spark partitions
Use the Glue merge operation to compact files post-write
Enable S3 multipart upload to combine files during write

questions.explanation:
Glue DynamicFrames are backed by Spark DataFrames. Using .toDF().coalesce(N) (or .repartition(N)) reduces the number of Spark output partitions before writing, directly controlling the number of output files. For example, coalesce(100) outputs ~100 Parquet files. S3 compaction with AWS Glue Merge can also help for Delta/Hudi tables, but for standard Parquet output, partition control before write is the correct approach.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/author-job.html

questions.stem: You need to replicate changes from an on-premises Oracle database to Amazon S3 in near real-time using change data capture (CDC). Which AWS service is purpose-built for this?

options.text: AWS Database Migration Service (DMS) with CDC enabled
AWS DataSync
Amazon Kinesis Data Streams with a custom Oracle listener
AWS Glue with JDBC connection polling every 5 minutes

questions.explanation:
AWS DMS supports CDC by reading the database transaction log (Oracle redo log) and replicating INSERTs, UPDATEs, and DELETEs to a target. DMS can write CDC events directly to S3 in CSV or Parquet format. DataSync is for file/object storage migration (bulk copy), not database CDC. Glue polling is not CDC — it misses deletes and runs in batch intervals, not near real-time.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/streams/latest/dev/introduction.html

questions.stem: Which AWS service provides a serverless SQL interface to query data directly in Amazon S3 without loading it into a database?

options.text: Amazon Athena
Amazon Redshift
AWS Glue DataBrew
Amazon EMR

questions.explanation:
Amazon Athena is a serverless, interactive query service that uses standard SQL to analyze data directly in Amazon S3. It requires no ETL or data loading — you define a schema via AWS Glue Data Catalog and query immediately. You pay per query based on data scanned. Redshift requires data to be loaded into the cluster (though Redshift Spectrum can also query S3). Glue DataBrew is for data preparation, not SQL querying.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/components-overview.html

questions.stem: A data engineering team uses AWS Glue to transform data. They want to implement unit testing for Glue ETL scripts locally before deploying to AWS. Which TWO approaches support local testing of Glue scripts? (Select TWO)

options.text: AWS Glue Interactive Sessions in a Jupyter notebook
AWS Glue local development libraries (aws-glue-libs) with Docker
Testing directly in the AWS Glue console with live production data
Amazon EMR Serverless with Glue job templates

questions.explanation:
AWS Glue Interactive Sessions provide a notebook-based development experience connected to a serverless Glue session, enabling iterative development and testing without a full job run. AWS Glue provides aws-glue-libs as a Docker image for local development — you can run and test Glue PySpark scripts on a local machine. Testing in the production console with live data is a bad practice. EMR Serverless is a separate service and does not support Glue scripts natively.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/author-job.html

questions.stem: You are using Amazon Data Firehose to deliver JSON records to S3. You want records to be automatically converted to Parquet and partitioned by year/month/day without writing custom code. Which Firehose feature provides this?

options.text: Firehose dynamic partitioning with data format conversion (Apache Parquet via AWS Glue schema)
An AWS Lambda transformation function that converts JSON to Parquet before delivery
An S3 lifecycle policy that converts JSON files to Parquet after delivery
AWS Glue ETL job triggered by S3 event notification after Firehose delivery

questions.explanation:
Amazon Data Firehose supports native data format conversion (JSON to Parquet or ORC) using a schema defined in AWS Glue Data Catalog. Dynamic partitioning allows extracting key values from JSON records and using them to partition S3 output (e.g., year=2024/month=01/day=15). Both features are built-in to Firehose, requiring no Lambda functions or post-processing jobs.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/components-overview.html

questions.stem: Your data lake on S3 has grown to 5 PB with files from multiple years. Most queries access only the last 90 days of data. How should you configure S3 to minimize storage costs for older data?

options.text: Enable S3 Intelligent-Tiering for all objects
Create S3 lifecycle policies to transition objects to S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval after 90 days and Glacier Deep Archive after 1 year
Delete objects older than 1 year
Enable S3 Requester Pays for older data

questions.explanation:
S3 lifecycle policies automatically transition objects to lower-cost storage classes based on age. Transitioning to S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval (millisecond retrieval for occasional access) after 90 days and Glacier Deep Archive (lowest cost, hours retrieval) after 1 year provides significant cost savings for rarely accessed historical data while keeping recent data in S3 Standard for fast queries. S3 Intelligent-Tiering also works but has per-object monitoring fees.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/Welcome.html

questions.stem: An Amazon Redshift cluster experiences slow query performance on a table with 500 million rows. EXPLAIN plan shows data redistribution between nodes. The table is queried with joins on customer_id. What should you do?

options.text: Set the distribution style to ALL for the table
Set the distribution key (DISTKEY) to customer_id to co-locate join data on the same node
Change the sort key to customer_id
Enable result caching for all queries

questions.explanation:
In Amazon Redshift, when two large tables are joined and do not have matching distribution keys, Redshift must redistribute (move) data across nodes — a costly network operation. Setting DISTKEY to customer_id on both tables ensures rows with the same customer_id are co-located on the same node slice, eliminating redistribution. DISTSTYLE ALL copies the entire table to every node (only suitable for small dimension tables). Sort keys improve scan efficiency but not join redistribution.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/redshift/latest/dg/welcome.html

questions.stem: You need to implement a data lakehouse on AWS with support for ACID transactions, time travel queries, and schema evolution on S3-backed tables. Which table format should you use?

options.text: Plain Parquet files with Glue Data Catalog
Apache Iceberg or Apache Hudi with Glue Data Catalog
Amazon Redshift external tables via Redshift Spectrum
AWS Lake Formation governed tables

questions.explanation:
Apache Iceberg and Apache Hudi are open table formats that add ACID transactions, time travel (query historical snapshots), schema evolution, and partition evolution on top of S3-stored Parquet files. Both are fully supported by AWS Glue, Athena, and EMR. AWS Lake Formation governed tables also provide some of these capabilities but with Lake Formation-specific APIs. Plain Parquet has none of these features. Redshift Spectrum is an S3 query layer, not a table format.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/components-overview.html

questions.stem: An Amazon DynamoDB table stores user sessions with TTL set on each item. The table uses on-demand capacity mode. Queries filter on a non-key attribute (last_active_date). Performance is poor for these queries. What should you do?

options.text: Enable DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) for all read operations
Create a Global Secondary Index (GSI) with last_active_date as the partition key
Switch to provisioned capacity mode with auto-scaling
Use DynamoDB Streams to export data to Athena for analytical queries

questions.explanation:
DynamoDB requires queries to use the primary key or a secondary index. Filtering on a non-key attribute requires a full table scan, which is slow and expensive. A Global Secondary Index (GSI) with last_active_date as the partition key allows efficient queries on that attribute. DAX improves read latency for point lookups but does not fix the scan problem. The capacity mode does not affect query patterns.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/athena/latest/ug/what-is.html

questions.stem: Which Amazon Redshift feature allows you to query data directly in S3 without loading it into Redshift, using the same SQL interface?

options.text: Redshift Data Sharing
Redshift Spectrum
Redshift Serverless
Redshift COPY command

questions.explanation:
Amazon Redshift Spectrum extends Redshift's SQL querying capability to data stored in S3, using the Glue Data Catalog as the schema registry. Queries can join Spectrum external tables with Redshift local tables. Data does not need to be loaded — Spectrum pushes query predicates down to the S3 layer. Redshift Data Sharing shares data between Redshift clusters. COPY loads data into Redshift.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/components-overview.html

questions.stem: You need a fully managed NoSQL database optimized for storing and querying semi-structured JSON documents with flexible schemas, supporting both document and key-value access patterns. Which AWS service is best suited?

options.text: Amazon DynamoDB
Amazon DocumentDB (with MongoDB compatibility)
Amazon Aurora
Amazon ElastiCache

questions.explanation:
Amazon DocumentDB is a managed document database service compatible with MongoDB APIs, designed for storing, querying, and indexing JSON documents. It supports flexible schemas, rich document queries, and aggregation pipelines. DynamoDB is key-value with optional document features but limited query flexibility. Aurora is a relational (SQL) database. ElastiCache is an in-memory cache.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazondynamodb/latest/developerguide/Introduction.html

questions.stem: You need to orchestrate a multi-step data pipeline that includes Glue jobs, Lambda functions, and EMR jobs with dependencies between steps. Failures should trigger retries. Which AWS service is designed for this?

options.text: Amazon EventBridge scheduled rules
AWS Step Functions
Amazon SQS with Lambda triggers
AWS Batch

questions.explanation:
AWS Step Functions is a serverless workflow orchestration service that allows you to define state machines with sequential, parallel, and branching steps. It natively integrates with Glue, Lambda, EMR, DynamoDB, SageMaker, and more. Built-in retry logic, error handling, and visual workflow monitoring make it ideal for complex data pipelines. EventBridge is for event-driven scheduling, not multi-step orchestration with dependencies.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/author-job.html

questions.stem: An AWS Glue job uses job bookmarks to track processed S3 files. After fixing a bug in the transformation logic, you need to reprocess all files from the beginning. What should you do?

options.text: Delete and recreate the Glue job
Reset the Glue job bookmark to clear the bookmark state, then run the job
Disable job bookmarks and re-enable them after reprocessing
Modify the job's MaxRetries setting to force full reprocessing

questions.explanation:
AWS Glue job bookmarks store the state of what has been processed so that subsequent runs only process new data. To reprocess all data, you reset the bookmark state via the Glue console (Actions > Reset job bookmark) or via the AWS CLI (aws glue reset-job-bookmark). After reset, the next job run processes all available data from the beginning. Deleting and recreating the job is unnecessarily destructive.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/author-job.html

questions.stem: Your team runs multiple Apache Spark jobs on Amazon EMR for daily data processing. Jobs occasionally fail due to spot instance interruption. What should you configure to make the cluster resilient to spot interruptions at lowest cost?

options.text: Use only On-Demand instances for all EMR node types
Use On-Demand instances for the master and core nodes; use Spot instances for task nodes only
Use Spot instances for all nodes including master and core
Enable EMR Serverless which automatically handles instance interruptions

questions.explanation:
EMR best practice is to use On-Demand instances for master (cluster management) and core nodes (HDFS storage), which are critical for cluster stability. Task nodes only execute compute and have no HDFS data, so they are safe for Spot instances — if interrupted, running tasks are rescheduled to other nodes. Using Spot for core nodes risks data loss if HDFS blocks are lost. All On-Demand eliminates cost savings. EMR Serverless is a separate product that eliminates cluster management but has different pricing.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/emr/latest/ManagementGuide/emr-what-is-emr.html

questions.stem: A daily ETL pipeline fails intermittently. CloudWatch Logs show the Glue job completing successfully, but downstream Athena queries return stale data. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: Athena result caching is returning old query results from a previous execution
The Glue Data Catalog table was not updated after writing new S3 partitions; run a Glue Crawler or call MSCK REPAIR TABLE
S3 eventual consistency is delaying object visibility to Athena
Glue DynamicFrames do not flush buffered writes to S3 immediately

questions.explanation:
When a Glue job writes new data to new S3 partitions, the Glue Data Catalog is not automatically updated unless explicitly configured. Athena queries the Glue catalog for partition metadata, so new partitions are invisible to Athena until discovered. Running a Glue Crawler (or executing MSCK REPAIR TABLE / ALTER TABLE ADD PARTITION in Athena) updates the catalog with new partitions. Note: S3 has strong read-after-write consistency since 2020, so option **C** is outdated.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/add-crawler.html

questions.stem: You need to monitor an Amazon Kinesis Data Stream and automatically scale the number of shards based on incoming data volume. What is the recommended approach?

options.text: Enable Kinesis Data Streams On-Demand mode which automatically scales shards
Create a CloudWatch alarm on the IncomingBytes metric and trigger a Lambda to call UpdateShardCount
Use Kinesis Scaling Utilities (open-source) deployed on EC2
Kinesis shards are fixed and cannot be scaled after stream creation

questions.explanation:
Kinesis Data Streams On-Demand mode automatically manages shard count based on incoming throughput, scaling up to handle traffic spikes and scaling down during low-throughput periods. This eliminates the need for manual shard management or custom auto-scaling code. On-Demand mode is the simplest approach for variable workloads. The manual Lambda + UpdateShardCount approach works but adds operational complexity.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/streams/latest/dev/introduction.html

questions.stem: You need to create a CloudWatch dashboard that shows the number of records processed per minute by a Kinesis Data Stream, Glue job DPU utilization, and S3 PUT requests. What metrics must you configure?

options.text: All metrics are automatically available; create a CloudWatch dashboard and add them as widgets
Kinesis and S3 metrics are in CloudWatch by default; Glue job metrics require enabling Glue job observability metrics
All three services require custom metric publishing via the CloudWatch Logs agent
S3 request metrics must be enabled at the bucket level; Kinesis and Glue metrics are automatic

questions.explanation:
Kinesis Data Streams publishes metrics (IncomingRecords, GetRecords.IteratorAgeMilliseconds, etc.) to CloudWatch automatically. S3 request metrics (PutRequests, GetRequests) must be enabled per bucket under S3 > Metrics > Request metrics (they are not enabled by default). AWS Glue DPU utilization metrics require enabling the Continuous Job Metrics / Observability Metrics feature.

Option **D** is partially correct about S3 but misses the Glue requirement.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/author-job.html

questions.stem: An S3 bucket stores sensitive customer PII data. Data engineers should be able to read and write objects, but must not be able to delete them. How do you enforce this using IAM?

options.text: Grant s3:GetObject and s3:PutObject permissions; explicitly deny s3:DeleteObject
Grant s3:GetObject and s3:PutObject; omit s3:DeleteObject from the policy (implicit deny)
Enable S3 Object Lock in COMPLIANCE mode on the bucket
Use an S3 bucket policy that allows only the data engineer IAM role

questions.explanation:
In IAM, not granting an action is an implicit deny, which works when no other policy grants the permission. However, if another policy (e.g., an admin policy or a broader S3 policy) grants s3:DeleteObject, the implicit deny is overridden. Adding an explicit Deny for s3:DeleteObject ensures the restriction holds regardless of other permissions — IAM explicit denies always override any allow. For critical data protection, explicit deny is the correct approach.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/Welcome.html

questions.stem: Your data lake contains multiple S3 buckets across accounts. You need a centralized service to catalog all datasets, manage access permissions, and enforce column-level security for sensitive columns. Which AWS service provides this?

options.text: AWS Glue Data Catalog with IAM resource policies
AWS Lake Formation
Amazon Macie
AWS IAM Identity Center

questions.explanation:
AWS Lake Formation provides centralized data lake governance: it catalogs datasets (via Glue Catalog integration), manages table and column-level access permissions, applies row-level and cell-level security, and enforces permissions across Athena, Redshift Spectrum, Glue, and EMR. Column-level security is a key Lake Formation feature — you can grant access to specific columns of a table. Macie is for PII discovery, not access control.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/components-overview.html

questions.stem: An AWS Glue job must read from an RDS database in a private VPC subnet (no public access). What configuration is required?

options.text: Configure the Glue job to use a Glue Connection with VPC, subnet, and security group settings pointing to the RDS subnet
Create a VPC endpoint for AWS Glue in the RDS VPC
Attach a NAT Gateway to the Glue job's network configuration
Enable RDS public accessibility and restrict access with a security group rule for the Glue IP range

questions.explanation:
AWS Glue Connections define network connectivity parameters (VPC ID, subnet ID, security group) for private data sources. When a Glue job references a Connection, it runs in an ENI (Elastic Network Interface) attached to the specified subnet, allowing direct private access to RDS in the same VPC. The security group must allow inbound connections from the Glue ENI's security group. This keeps the RDS database private.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/author-job.html

questions.stem: You need to ensure that sensitive data stored in S3 is automatically classified and that any unencrypted objects are detected. Which AWS service provides automated sensitive data discovery and encryption compliance monitoring for S3?

options.text: AWS Security Hub with S3 security standards
Amazon Macie
AWS Config with s3-bucket-server-side-encryption-enabled rule
Both B and C together

questions.explanation:
Amazon Macie uses machine learning to automatically discover and classify sensitive data (PII, financial data, credentials) in S3 buckets. AWS Config's s3-bucket-server-side-encryption-enabled managed rule continuously evaluates whether S3 buckets enforce server-side encryption and flags non-compliant buckets. Using both provides comprehensive coverage: Macie for content-level sensitive data classification, AWS Config for encryption compliance. Security Hub aggregates findings from both.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/Welcome.html

questions.stem: A Redshift cluster query logs contain SQL statements that include raw customer email addresses. You need to ensure that sensitive data is masked in query logs. Which approach implements this?

options.text: Enable Redshift Dynamic Data Masking policies on sensitive columns
Encrypt the Redshift cluster at rest with KMS
Use Redshift column-level access control to restrict email column access
Enable CloudWatch Logs encryption for Redshift audit logs

questions.explanation:
Amazon Redshift Dynamic Data Masking (DDM) allows defining masking policies that substitute sensitive column values with redacted or tokenized values based on the user's role. Users without full access see masked values in query results — and importantly, masked values appear in query results rather than raw PII appearing in logs. Column-level access control would block the column entirely (access error), not mask it. Encryption at rest protects stored data but not query log content.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/redshift/latest/dg/welcome.html

questions.stem: You need to build a real-time data pipeline that reads from a Kinesis Data Stream, enriches each event by looking up reference data in DynamoDB, and writes results to S3. Which service provides the lowest operational overhead?

options.text: Amazon Kinesis Data Analytics (Apache Flink)
AWS Lambda with Kinesis trigger
Amazon EMR Streaming with Spark Structured Streaming
Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose with Lambda transformation

questions.explanation:
Kinesis Data Firehose with a Lambda transformation is the lowest-overhead approach: Firehose handles reading from Kinesis, buffering, and writing to S3; the Lambda function performs DynamoDB lookups for enrichment. No cluster management or Flink/Spark expertise is needed. Kinesis Data Analytics (Flink) provides more sophisticated streaming capabilities but requires Flink knowledge. EMR requires cluster management. Lambda-only with Kinesis trigger is more operationally complex for fan-out delivery.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kinesisanalytics/latest/dev/what-is.html

questions.stem: An Amazon Redshift table query takes 30 minutes. EXPLAIN shows a step with DS_DIST_ALL_INNER, indicating one table is redistributed to all nodes for every join. The redistributed table has 10 million rows and is 500 MB. What is the recommended fix?

options.text: Set DISTSTYLE EVEN on the large redistributed table
Set DISTSTYLE ALL on the 500 MB table so it is replicated to all nodes permanently
Increase the Redshift cluster node count
Enable Concurrency Scaling to handle the redistribution load

questions.explanation:
DS_DIST_ALL_INNER means the inner table is redistributed to all nodes at query time. At 500 MB, this is within the practical limit for DISTSTYLE ALL, which replicates the table to every node permanently. This eliminates the redistribution step entirely. DISTSTYLE ALL is appropriate for tables under ~1 GB that are frequently joined. DISTSTYLE EVEN distributes rows evenly but does not prevent redistribution during joins.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/redshift/latest/dg/welcome.html

questions.stem: You need to implement data quality checks on incoming S3 data before it is processed by Glue. Checks include: null count threshold, value range validation, and uniqueness. Which AWS service provides managed data quality rules for Glue data?

options.text: AWS Glue Data Quality (DQDL rules)
Amazon DataZone data quality rules
Custom Lambda function with Great Expectations library
AWS Config with custom rules for S3 data validation

questions.explanation:
AWS Glue Data Quality uses the Data Quality Definition Language (DQDL) to define declarative quality rules (ColumnValues, IsUnique, RowCount, etc.). Rules can be applied to Glue tables or embedded in Glue ETL jobs as a pre/post-processing step. Results are stored as quality scores and can fail the job or trigger alerts. This is the native AWS approach for Glue-based data quality without third-party tools.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/Welcome.html

questions.stem: An AWS Glue Spark job processes 10 TB of data daily. After optimizing the Spark code, average job duration is still 4 hours. The data is read from S3 in Parquet format with 400,000 small files (25 MB each). What single change would most reduce job duration?

options.text: Increase the number of DPUs to 200
Compact the small Parquet files into larger files (e.g., 512 MB each) and update the S3 prefixes
Enable Glue Auto Scaling to dynamically adjust DPUs
Switch from Parquet to ORC format for better compression

questions.explanation:
400,000 files of 25 MB each creates extreme small file overhead: Spark creates one task per file, causing task scheduling overhead and frequent S3 LIST/GET API calls. With 400K files, even with many workers, the task scheduling overhead dominates. Compacting to ~20,000 files of 512 MB each reduces the task count by 20x, dramatically reducing scheduling overhead and S3 API calls. This is the primary cause and fix. Increasing DPUs does not help if the bottleneck is small file overhead.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/Welcome.html

questions.stem: Your organization requires that all data in S3 used by the data lake be encrypted with customer-managed keys that you can rotate annually. Which encryption configuration satisfies this requirement?

options.text: Enable S3 default encryption with SSE-S3 (AES-256)
Enable S3 default encryption with SSE-KMS using a customer-managed KMS key (CMK)
Use client-side encryption with your own key before uploading to S3
Enable S3 Block Public Access and encrypt using SSE-C

questions.explanation:
SSE-KMS with a customer-managed key (CMK) allows annual key rotation via AWS KMS key rotation policies or manual rotation. You retain control over the key — you can disable, revoke, or audit all usage in CloudTrail. SSE-S3 uses AWS-managed keys with no customer control over rotation timing. SSE-C requires customers to manage keys outside AWS, losing KMS audit trail. Client-side encryption adds operational complexity.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/Welcome.html

questions.stem: Amazon Athena queries on a partitioned S3 table are scanning more data than expected, even though partition pruning should apply. The partition filter is applied using a WHERE clause. What could cause this?

options.text: The WHERE clause uses a function on the partition column (e.g., YEAR(date_col) = 2024) preventing pruning
Athena does not support partition pruning
The S3 bucket versioning is enabled, causing Athena to scan all object versions
The Glue Crawler set the wrong data type for the partition column

questions.explanation:
Partition pruning in Athena (and most query engines) requires the filter to reference the partition column directly without wrapping it in a function. YEAR(date_col) = 2024 applies a function to the partition column, preventing the engine from pushing the filter to partition metadata — it must scan all partitions and evaluate the function on each row. The fix is to use date_col BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-12-31' or date_col >= '2024-01-01' AND date_col < '2025-01-01'.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/add-crawler.html

questions.stem: You need to transfer 50 TB of data from an on-premises data center to Amazon S3 over a dedicated 10 Gbps AWS Direct Connect connection. Approximately how long will the transfer take at maximum throughput?

options.text: Approximately 11 hours
Approximately 2 days
Approximately 5 days
AWS Direct Connect cannot be used for S3 data transfer

questions.explanation:
50 TB = 50 × 1024 × 8 Gbits = 409,600 Gbits. At 10 Gbps: 409,600 / 10 = 40,960 seconds ≈ 11.4 hours at full theoretical throughput. In practice, usable throughput is ~60-80% of nominal, so expect 14-18 hours. option **A** (11 hours) represents the theoretical maximum, which is what exam questions typically test. AWS Direct Connect is absolutely used for S3 data transfer via the S3 gateway endpoint or virtual private interface.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/Welcome.html

questions.stem: A Step Functions state machine orchestrates a data pipeline. One state makes an API call to a third-party service that takes 20-30 minutes to complete. The state machine should wait for the result without polling. Which Step Functions pattern should you use?

options.text: Task state with a polling loop (Wait + Choice state)
Task state with .waitForTaskToken, where the third-party service calls back with the token when complete
Parallel state with a 30-minute timeout
Express Workflow with synchronous execution

questions.explanation:
The .waitForTaskToken pattern is designed for exactly this use case. Step Functions pauses the workflow and provides a task token to the worker (in this case, passed to the third-party API call). The external service performs its work and calls back to the Step Functions API (SendTaskSuccess/SendTaskFailure) with the token, resuming the workflow. This avoids polling and charges, and the workflow can wait up to 1 year. Polling loops work but are inefficient and increase state machine costs.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/step-functions/latest/dg/welcome.html

questions.stem: You need to perform time travel queries on a Delta Lake table stored in S3 to recover data that was accidentally deleted. You are using Amazon Athena. What must be true to support this?

options.text: Athena does not support Delta Lake time travel; use Amazon EMR with Spark instead
The Delta table transaction log (_delta_log) must be intact, and Athena must query using the VERSION AS OF or TIMESTAMP AS OF syntax
Enable S3 Versioning on the Delta table bucket and use the S3 console to restore previous file versions
Delta Lake time travel requires Databricks Runtime; it is not available on AWS

questions.explanation:
Amazon Athena supports Apache Iceberg natively with time travel queries, and also supports Delta Lake tables (with the Athena for Apache Spark engine or via the native Delta connector). Delta Lake's transaction log (_delta_log) tracks all operations with checksums. Time travel uses VERSION AS OF <n> or TIMESTAMP AS OF '<ts>' to query historical snapshots. The transaction log must be intact. S3 versioning is a separate mechanism and does not provide Delta-aware time travel semantics.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/athena/latest/ug/what-is.html

questions.stem: You need to audit all API calls made to your S3 buckets and Glue Data Catalog for compliance purposes, including who accessed what data and when. Which AWS service provides this audit trail?

options.text: Amazon CloudWatch Logs
AWS CloudTrail
S3 Server Access Logs
AWS Config

questions.explanation:
AWS CloudTrail records all AWS API calls — including S3 data events (GetObject, PutObject, DeleteObject) and Glue Data Catalog API calls — with identity (IAM user/role), timestamp, source IP, and request parameters. CloudTrail logs can be stored in S3, analyzed with Athena, and used for compliance auditing. S3 Server Access Logs record S3 requests but are not consolidated across services. AWS Config tracks configuration changes, not API access patterns.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/components-overview.html

questions.stem: You want to be notified when an AWS Glue job fails within 5 minutes of the failure. Which configuration achieves this?

options.text: Create a CloudWatch alarm on the glue.driver.jvm.heap.used metric with an SNS notification
Create an Amazon EventBridge rule that matches Glue job state change events with FAILED status and triggers an SNS topic
Enable Glue job email notifications in the Glue console notification settings
Configure an S3 event notification on the Glue logs bucket

questions.explanation:
AWS Glue publishes job state change events to Amazon EventBridge automatically. Creating an EventBridge rule with the pattern {source: 'aws.glue', detail-type: 'Glue Job State Change', detail: {state: ['FAILED']}} and targeting an SNS topic delivers near-immediate failure notifications. This approach is event-driven (no polling delay) and is the recommended AWS pattern for Glue job monitoring and alerting.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/author-job.html

questions.stem: You need to migrate a 10 TB MySQL database from on-premises to Amazon Aurora MySQL with minimal downtime. The database serves an application that cannot tolerate more than 30 seconds of downtime during the final cutover. Which approach is best?

options.text: Stop the application, run mysqldump, transfer the dump to Aurora, restore, then start the application
Use AWS DMS with full load followed by CDC replication; cut over when Aurora is caught up to within seconds of the source
Use AWS DataSync to replicate MySQL binary log files to S3, then load into Aurora
Create an Aurora Read Replica of the on-premises MySQL and promote it

questions.explanation:
AWS DMS performs a full load of the existing data, then switches to CDC (reading MySQL binary logs) to replicate ongoing changes. The application continues running during migration. When Aurora is within seconds of the source's current position, you stop writes to the source, wait for DMS to apply the remaining changes, and cut over — all within seconds to minutes. mysqldump would require much longer downtime for 10 TB. Aurora read replicas cannot be created from on-premises MySQL.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/dms/latest/userguide/Welcome.html

questions.stem: You need to implement an Amazon Redshift cluster that serves both interactive BI queries from analysts and bulk nightly ETL loads. Both workloads must not interfere with each other. Which Redshift feature isolates these workloads?

options.text: Redshift Query Monitoring Rules (QMR)
Redshift Workload Management (WLM) with separate queues for BI queries and ETL jobs
Create separate Redshift clusters for BI and ETL
Enable Redshift Concurrency Scaling for BI queries

questions.explanation:
Redshift Workload Management (WLM) allows defining query queues with allocated memory percentages and concurrency slots. You create a BI queue with high concurrency and a query timeout, and an ETL queue with high memory allocation. Queries are routed to queues based on user groups or query groups. This isolates workloads on the same cluster, ensuring ETL bulk loads don't consume resources meant for interactive BI queries.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/redshift/latest/dg/welcome.html

questions.stem: A data engineer's IAM role has s3:GetObject permission on a specific S3 bucket. An S3 bucket policy explicitly denies s3:GetObject for all principals. Can the data engineer read objects from this bucket?

options.text: Yes, the IAM role allow takes precedence over the bucket policy deny
No, explicit denies in any policy always override allows in other policies
Yes, if the data engineer is the bucket owner
It depends on whether the IAM role has an attached permissions boundary

questions.explanation:
AWS IAM policy evaluation follows a strict hierarchy: explicit denies always override allows, regardless of which policy contains them. If the S3 bucket policy explicitly denies s3:GetObject for all principals (including the data engineer's role), the explicit deny cannot be overridden by an IAM role allow. The data engineer cannot access the bucket. The only exception is if the deny includes a condition that exempts certain principals (e.g., aws:PrincipalArn condition), but an unconditional explicit deny is absolute.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/Welcome.html

questions.stem: You need to implement data lineage tracking for your AWS data lake, showing how data flows from source S3 buckets through Glue ETL jobs to final Redshift tables. Which AWS service provides native lineage tracking?

options.text: AWS CloudTrail with Athena analysis
Amazon DataZone
AWS Glue Data Catalog with lineage enabled
Amazon DataZone or AWS Glue with Apache Atlas integration

questions.explanation:
Amazon DataZone provides data lineage visualization showing how data flows through your AWS data estate. It integrates with Glue, Redshift, Athena, and S3 to automatically capture and display lineage. AWS Glue Data Catalog stores metadata but does not natively visualize lineage graphs. CloudTrail captures API calls but not data flow lineage. DataZone is the current AWS-native lineage solution as of 2024.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/components-overview.html
